runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 5 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income estimates
CBO microsimulation assumptions for labor supply elasticities
Immervoll et al. (2007) - EUROMOD tax-benefit microsimulation framework
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners typically have low but positive substitution elasticities. For middle-decile (decile 5) primary earners, the literature and standard microsimulation calibrations generally place the compensated/substitution elasticity in the range of 0.1–0.25. Primary earners are less responsive than secondary earners, and middle-income workers tend to have modest responses. CBO and similar U.S. microsimulation models often use values around 0.1–0.2 for this group. Gruber and Saez's taxable income elasticity work and related calibrations anchor my belief toward ~0.15, with uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.35 to account for model specification differences.

citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income estimates

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners (typically men or main household earners) generally have low labor supply elasticities. The substitution elasticity for this group at the median of the earnings distribution (decile 5) is typically parameterized in U.S. microsimulation models in the range of 0.1–0.25. runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 5 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of Taxable Income
CBO microsimulation assumptions for labor supply elasticities
Immervoll et al. (2007) - EUROMOD tax-benefit microsimulation elasticity calibrations
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners (typically men or main household earners) are generally found to have low substitution elasticities in the labor supply literature, typically in the range of 0.1–0.3. For the median decile (decile 5), workers are solidly in the labor force with moderate earnings, so their intensive-margin wage elasticity is expected to be modest. U.S. microsimulation models (e.g., CBO, TAXSIM-linked models) typically calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities in the 0.1–0.25 range for middle-income deciles, drawing on estimates from Gruber and Saez and related taxable income elasticity literature. Decile 5 sits in the middle of the distribution where neither very low nor very high effective marginal tax rate changes dominate, suggesting a value near 0.15. Uncertainty spans roughly 0.05 to 0.35, reflecting variation across model specifications and empirical estimates.

Decile 5 sits in the middle of the distribution where neither very low nor very high effective marginal tax rate changes dominate, suggesting a value near 0.15. Uncertainty spans roughly 0.05 to 0.35, reflecting variation across model specifications and empirical estimates."}

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners (typically men or main household earners) have relatively low labor supply elasticities. The substitution elasticity for the middle of the earnings distribution (decile 5) in microsimulation models is typically calibrated in the range of 0.1–0.25. Gruber and Saez find compensated elasticities of taxable income around 0.12–0.40 depending on specification, but pure hours/participation substitution elasticities for primary earners tend to be at the lower end. CBO and similar microsimulation frameworks often use values around 0.10–0.25 for prime-age primary earners. Decile 5 is squarely in the middle of the distribution where labor supply is relatively inelastic compared to lower deciles (where extensive margin matters more) or higher deciles (where taxable income responses may be larger). I center my estimate at 0.15 with moderate uncertainty.

runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners (typically men or higher-earning spouses) at the median of the earnings distribution (decile 5), substitution elasticities used in U.S. tax-benefit microsimulations are generally modest. Decile 5 is squarely in the middle where elasticities tend to be lower than at the tails (very low earners may have higher participation elasticities, very high earners may have higher taxable income elasticities). I anchor on approximately 0.15 as the median, with meaningful uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and calibration choices.
